content.text: Hydrocarbons are organic compounds composed entirely of carbon (C) and hydrogen (H) atoms. They are the simplest class of organic compounds and serve as the basis of organic chemistry.

• **Definition:** Hydrocarbons with only single bonds (C–C).
• **General Formula:** C$_n$H$_{2n+2}$
• **Properties:** Saturated, chemically less reactive, non-polar, insoluble in water.
• **Reactions:** React with halogens under UV light (substitution reaction).
• **Examples:** Methane (CH$_4$), Ethane (C$_2$H$_6$), Propane (C$_3$H$_8$).

• **Definition:** Hydrocarbons with one or more carbon-carbon double bonds (C=C).
• **General Formula:** C$_n$H$_{2n}$ (for one double bond).
• **Properties:** Unsaturated, more reactive than alkanes.
• **Reactions:** Undergo addition reactions.
• **Examples:** Ethene (C$_2$H$_4$), Propene (C$_3$H$_6$).

• **Definition:** Hydrocarbons with one or more carbon-carbon triple bonds (C$\equiv$C).
• **General Formula:** C$_n$H$_{2n-2}$ (for one triple bond).
• **Properties:** Highly reactive, undergo addition reactions.
• **Examples:** Ethyne (C$_2$H$_2$), Propyne (C$_3$H$_4$).

• **Definition:** Hydrocarbons containing benzene rings.
• **General Formula:** C$_6$H$_6$ for benzene.
• **Properties:** Stable due to resonance (delocalized $\pi$ electrons); undergo substitution reactions rather than addition.
• **Examples:** Benzene (C$_6$H$_6$), Toluene (C$_6$H$_5$CH$_3$).

| Hydrocarbon | Typical Reaction | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Alkane | Substitution | CH$_4$ + Cl$_2$ $\rightarrow$ CH$_3$Cl + HCl |
| Alkene | Addition | C$_2$H$_4$ + H$_2$ $\rightarrow$ C$_2$H$_6$ |
| Alkyne | Addition | C$_2$H$_2$ + Br$_2$ $\rightarrow$ C$_2$H$_2$Br$_2$ |
| Aromatic | Substitution | C$_6$H$_6$ + Br$_2$ $\rightarrow$ C$_6$H$_5$Br + HBr |

• **Natural sources:** Petroleum, natural gas, coal.
• **Manufactured:** By chemical synthesis in laboratories.

content.label: 💡 Summary
content.text:
• Hydrocarbons consist only of C and H.
• Saturated (alkanes) have single bonds and are less reactive.
• Unsaturated (alkenes/alkynes) have double/triple bonds and are more reactive.
• Aromatic compounds are stable due to resonance.
